one_line_takeaway: GAAP forces an LLM agent to express each task as generated code and enforces confidentiality deterministically via information-flow control over that code plus persistent user-defined permission, disclosure-log, and annotation databases, blocking 100% of tested data-exfiltration prompt-injection attacks (versus up to 100% success for an undefended agent and partial leakage even through LLM-judge/Conseca-style defenses) while retaining utility close to comparable code-generation baselines.
key_finding: Privacy: on the 20-task benchmark's three injection attacks, GAAP blocked 100% of attacks (0% attack success) in all cases. NP-Agent (no protection) allowed 75% of SSN-leak attacks and 100% of applicable Phone-leak and SSN-swap attacks to succeed (applicability: 100% of tasks for SSN-leak, 38.9% for Phone-leak, 10% for SSN-swap). LLM-Judge allowed 15% of SSN-leak attacks to succeed; Conseca blocked all SSN-leak attacks but, like LLM-Judge, blocked only some Phone-leak attacks and neither blocked any applicable SSN-swap instances. Utility: on GAAP's own 20-task suite, NP-Agent had the highest utility (81.0%, 13/20 tasks solved on all runs), GAAP was second (76.0%, 11/20 on all runs), LLM-Judge 75.0% (12/20), Conseca 72.0% (11/20). On AgentDojo, NP-Agent scored 80.1%; GAAP and CaMeL (1 execution attempt) tied at 64.6%; CaMeL (10 execution attempts) reached 71.0%; LLM-Judge scored 69.8%; Conseca scored 45.3%. Cost: GAAP asked the user for permission 55 times on average across the suite (39 real/batched interactions) versus Conseca's 5; persisting the permission DB across tasks reduced queries from 85 to 55 (35% fewer) and real interactions from 58 to 39 (32% fewer). GAAP cost $0.52 per full benchmark-suite run in GPT-5 API tokens versus NP-Agent's $0.67 (GAAP has smaller, more consistent input token counts but larger output token counts, ~100 lines of generated code per task). GAAP's average end-to-end latency was only 13% higher than NP-Agent's, with IFC analysis (Pyre) adding up to 20 seconds per task.
core_contribution: The paper presents GAAP (Guaranteed Accounting for Agent Privacy), an execution environment for LLM-based AI agents that provides deterministic confidentiality guarantees for private user data. Rather than trusting the agent LLM, the user's prompt, or the model provider, GAAP requires the agent to generate a Python code artifact for each task, then applies Information Flow Control (IFC, via a modified Pyre static analysis) to that artifact to track how private data flows to the inputs of external (MCP) tool calls. Disclosures are checked against a user-controlled permission database before being allowed. GAAP's four novel components, layered on top of a standard IFC core, are: (1) a private data DB (a key-value store of the user's sensitive data, accessible only to generated code, never to the LLM without explicit permission), (2) a permission DB (allow/deny rows for pairs of private-data-type and external party, filled incrementally by asking the user), (3) a disclosure log that records every prior disclosure so GAAP can catch indirect/transitive re-disclosure of data across tool calls and across tasks separated in time, and (4) an annotation framework (JSON specs, meant to be open-sourced/audited) describing which external entity a given tool call reaches and which prior taints a tool's output can legitimately carry. GAAP also supports 'multi-shot' execution, where intermediate results can (with user permission) be shown back to the LLM so it can generate a follow-up code artifact, unlike prior single-shot code-generation systems such as CaMeL.
threat_model: The adversary observes the inputs (prompt, context) to the AI model, fully controls the outputs of the AI model (text, code, tool calls), and controls some or all external services the agent talks to. This explicitly covers prompt injection, agent hallucination, a malicious/compromised AI model provider, model poisoning, and untrusted tool/MCP providers -- no data or LLM output is trusted. GAAP's guarantee: under this adversary, private user data is disclosed only to parties the user has explicitly authorized via permission specifications; this holds deterministically and does not depend on an AI model correctly detecting attacks. Explicit non-goals: GAAP provides confidentiality only, not integrity (it does not stop a prompt injection from altering control flow, only from causing unauthorized data disclosure); it does not protect private data the user types directly into a prompt; it leaks prompt timing/order metadata to the adversary; and its disclosure-log protection only covers flows that go through GAAP (data a user manually uploads to a service outside GAAP is not tracked).
limitations: The paper is explicit about several limitations rather than claiming completeness. GAAP provides confidentiality only, not integrity -- it does not stop malicious control-flow changes that don't lead to disclosure. It offers no protection for private data placed directly in the user's prompt (the paper prescribes a workaround: reference data by key, not value). The disclosure log only tracks flows mediated by GAAP; data shared with a service outside GAAP is invisible to it. Annotations are trusted but unverified by GAAP itself -- their correctness depends on an external open-source vetting process the paper does not build or evaluate. The permission DB and disclosure log incur unbounded storage growth over time (the disclosure log 'can grow arbitrarily large'), with only future-work mitigations proposed (eviction, compaction). Utility is measured only against a 20-task custom suite plus AgentDojo, both of comparatively small size, and only with GPT-5 as the underlying model -- generality across models/agents is not evaluated. CaMeL's utility comparison uses a different threat model (control-flow integrity, not the untrusted-everything threat model GAAP targets), which the authors flag as an imperfect comparison. Author-proposed future work: automatic discovery of private data via a filesystem-scanning ISA (already partially implemented but only optionally enabled), reducing user permission burden via learned privacy personas (at the cost of losing determinism), and adding a prompt-scrubbing subsystem to also protect private data typed directly into prompts.

datasets_used: Custom 20-task benchmark suite (personal-to-enterprise domains) adapted from AgentBench, MCP-Bench, and AgentDojo, using 10 MCP servers with 48 tools
Three custom prompt-injection attacks: SSN-leak, Phone-leak, SSN-swap
AgentDojo task/attack suite (workspace, banking, Slack, travel domains) used for utility comparisons only
quantitative_results: GAAP: 0% attack success rate across SSN-leak, Phone-leak, and SSN-swap on the 20-task suite
NP-Agent: 75% SSN-leak success, 100% of applicable Phone-leak and SSN-swap cases succeed
LLM-Judge: 15% SSN-leak success; blocks some but not all Phone-leak; blocks 0% of applicable SSN-swap
Conseca: 0% SSN-leak success; blocks some but not all Phone-leak; blocks 0% of applicable SSN-swap
Utility on GAAP's own suite: NP-Agent 81.0%, GAAP 76.0%, LLM-Judge 75.0%, Conseca 72.0%
Utility on AgentDojo: NP-Agent 80.1%, CaMeL (10 exec.) 71.0%, LLM-Judge 69.8%, GAAP and CaMeL (1 exec.) tied 64.6%, Conseca 45.3%
User permission requests: GAAP averages 55 logical / 39 real (batched) requests per full suite run vs. Conseca's 5
Permission DB persistence reduces requests from 85 to 55 (35% decrease) and real interactions from 58 to 39 (32% decrease)
Cost per full benchmark run (GPT-5 pricing): GAAP $0.52 vs. NP-Agent $0.67
Latency: GAAP adds only 13% over NP-Agent on average; IFC (Pyre) analysis takes up to 20 seconds per task
Implementation size: ~3,500 LoC for GAAP core, ~2,600 LoC for benchmark suite
baselines_compared: LLM-Judge (second LLM judges each tool call using full untrusted context, similar to LLM guardrail systems)
Conseca (isolated LLM generates tool-call policy from trusted-only context)
CaMeL (single-shot code-generation IFC system with static policy; utility-only comparison on AgentDojo)
